fields.Text:
Peripheral hepatic mass with capsular retraction/delayed enhancement pattern. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Cholangiocarcinoma}}

1/7

Q3. Correct; difficulty: moderate. This case demonstrates the typical imaging features of a peripheral cholangiocarcinoma involving the left hepatic lobe.





Open review Q3

fields.Text: Peripheral cholangiocarcinoma typically presents as a {{c1::peripheral hepatic mass with delayed enhancement and capsular retraction}}.
fields.Extra:
Q3. Correct; difficulty: moderate. This case demonstrates the typical imaging features of a peripheral cholangiocarcinoma involving the left hepatic lobe.

fields.Text:
Postmenopausal bleeding with endometrial canal mass and feeding vessel. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Endometrial polyp}}
fields.Extra:
Q4. Correct; difficulty: hard. The color Doppler sonographic image demonstrates a broad-based mass within the endometrial canal (yellow arrow) with a prominent feeding vessel that may be seen extending to the polyp (green arrow).

fields.Text: An endometrial polyp may show a {{c1::single feeding vessel}} extending into an endometrial canal mass on Doppler US.

fields.Text:
Postpartum fever and RLQ pain with expanded right ovarian vein filling defect. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Ovarian vein thrombophlebitis}}
fields.Extra:
Q5. Correct; difficulty: easy. A prominent low-density filling defect is noted within an expanded right ovarian vein (blue arrow), suggesting ovarian vein thrombosis.

fields.Text: Postpartum fever/RLQ pain with an expanded ovarian vein filling defect suggests {{c1::ovarian vein thrombophlebitis}}.
fields.Extra:

{{c1::Intraosseous lipoma}}

1/2
fields.Extra:
Q6. Correct; difficulty: not listed. Intraosseous lipoma is a benign tumor that is usually well-circumscribed with a narrow zone of transition and predominantly radiolucent radiographic appearance.

fields.Text: Intraosseous lipoma is a benign bone tumor with {{c1::fat content, narrow zone of transition, and usually radiolucent appearance}}.

{{c1::Anterior choroidal artery}}

fields.Extra:
Q7. Incorrect; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Anterior choroidal artery infarcts involve posterior limb internal capsule and medial temporal involvement with characteristic serpentine shape.

fields.Text: Anterior choroidal artery infarcts classically involve the {{c1::posterior limb internal capsule and medial temporal region}}.
fields.Extra:

Fluoroscopy setup diagram. Lowest patient exposure occurs with which setup?

{{c1::Image receptor close to patient and x-ray tube farther away}}

Q8. Correct; difficulty: easy. It is crucial to understand the parameters that affect radiation dose to both the patient and the staff.

fields.Text: To reduce fluoroscopic patient exposure, keep the {{c1::image receptor close to the patient and the x-ray tube farther from the patient}}.

fields.Extra:

fields.Text:
Silicone breast implant MRI with right linguine sign. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Right intracapsular implant rupture}}
fields.Extra:
Q10. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Intracapsular rupture occurs when silicone leaks from the implant shell but remains within the fibrous capsule - key MRI signs include the linguine sign (collapsed shell as wavy lines within silicone) and subcapsular lines, with the linguine sign being most diagnostic.





Open review Q10

fields.Text: The most diagnostic MRI sign of intracapsular silicone implant rupture is the {{c1::linguine sign}}.
fields.Extra:
Q10. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Intracapsular rupture occurs when silicone leaks from the implant shell but remains within the fibrous capsule - key MRI signs include the linguine sign (collapsed shell as wavy lines within silicone) and subcapsular lines, with the linguine sign being most diagnostic.






Open review Q10

fields.Text:
Trauma hip CT after reduction with posterior acetabular lip injury. Dislocation mechanism?

{{c1::Posterior hip dislocation}}
fields.Extra:
Q11. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Posterior dislocations of the hip are the most common direction of dislocation for this joint, outnumbering anterior dislocations. Posterior dislocation may have associated fractures of the posterior lip of the acetabulum (as seen in this case) and/or injury to the acetabular labrum.




Open review Q11

fields.Text: Posterior hip dislocations are the most common hip dislocation and may injure the {{c1::posterior acetabular lip/labrum}}.
fields.Extra:
Q11. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Posterior dislocations of the hip are the most common direction of dislocation for this joint, outnumbering anterior dislocations. Posterior dislocation may have associated fractures of the posterior lip of the acetabulum (as seen in this case) and/or injury to the acetabular labrum.





Open review Q11

fields.Text: A wipe test uses a {{c1::well counter}} to measure removable radioactive contamination.
fields.Extra:
Q12. Correct; difficulty: hard. A well counter is a scintillation detector that uses a photomultiplier tube.




Open review Q12

fields.Text:
Child after ankle injury with central distal tibial physeal obliteration. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Physeal bar}}

1/2
fields.Extra:
Q13. Correct; difficulty: not listed. The sagittal SPGR MR (physeal sensitive sequence) shows a near-complete central obliteration of the distal tibial growth plate (yellow arrows).

fields.Text: A physeal bar is a {{c1::bony bridge across the growth plate}} after physeal injury.
fields.Extra:

fields.Text:
Acute scrotal pain with focal testicular lesion suspicious for focal orchitis. Next step?

{{c1::Follow-up ultrasound in 2-4 weeks}}

1/2
fields.Extra:
Q14. Incorrect; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Focal testicular lesions presenting with acute scrotal pain may represent focal orchitis mimicking malignancy, requiring follow-up ultrasound in 2 to 4 weeks to ensure complete resolution. Persistent lesions mandate urological evaluation and tumor marker assessment.

fields.Text:
Third-trimester US with fetal peritoneal calcifications. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Meconium peritonitis}}
fields.Extra:
Q16. Correct; difficulty: easy. Small hyperechoic foci are seen surrounding the liver compatible with peritoneal calcifications.




Open review Q16

fields.Text: Fetal meconium peritonitis can appear as {{c1::peritoneal calcifications}} on prenatal ultrasound.
fields.Extra:
Q16. Correct; difficulty: easy. Small hyperechoic foci are seen surrounding the liver compatible with peritoneal calcifications.

fields.Text:
Hemoptysis in otherwise healthy nonsmoker with blind-ending bronchial structure. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Cardiac bronchus}}
fields.Extra:
Q17. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Accessory cardiac bronchus causes hemoptysis because it is a blind-ending tubular structure where secretions accumulate and cannot clear, leading to chronic inflammation and hyper-microvascularization at the distal tip. The resulting fragile, inflamed tissue becomes prone to bleeding, making the stagnant secretion environment the underlying mechanism for hemoptysis in this congenital anomaly.





Open review Q17

fields.Text: An accessory cardiac bronchus can cause hemoptysis because it is {{c1::blind-ending with secretion stasis, inflammation, and fragile hypervascular tissue}}.
fields.Extra:
Q17. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Accessory cardiac bronchus causes hemoptysis because it is a blind-ending tubular structure where secretions accumulate and cannot clear, leading to chronic inflammation and hyper-microvascularization at the distal tip. The resulting fragile, inflamed tissue becomes prone to bleeding, making the stagnant secretion environment the underlying mechanism for hemoptysis in this congenital anomaly.






Open review Q17

fields.Text:
Infant with floor-of-mouth cystic lesion above the mylohyoid. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Oral ranula}}

1/2
fields.Extra:
Q18. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: The oral ranula is confined to the floor of the mouth above the mylohyoid muscle, whereas plunging ranula extends through or around the mylohyoid muscle into the neck, presenting as a submandibular or cervical swelling.




Open review Q18

fields.Text: An oral ranula is confined {{c1::above the mylohyoid muscle}}, while a plunging ranula extends into the neck.
fields.Extra:
Q18. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: The oral ranula is confined to the floor of the mouth above the mylohyoid muscle, whereas plunging ranula extends through or around the mylohyoid muscle into the neck, presenting as a submandibular or cervical swelling.

fields.Text:
Ovarian cancer with peritoneal spread beyond the pelvis. Stage?

{{c1::Stage III}}
fields.Extra:
Q19. Incorrect; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Stage III ovarian cancer involves peritoneal spread beyond the pelvis and/or retroperitoneal lymph node metastases. Imaging shows peritoneal implants, ascites, and possible lymphadenopathy - findings that indicate need for neoadjuvant chemotherapy rather than primary cytoreductive surgery if disease burden is extensive.





Open review Q19

fields.Text: Stage III ovarian cancer involves {{c1::peritoneal spread beyond the pelvis and/or retroperitoneal lymph node metastases}}.

fields.Text:
Prenatal US with large echogenic intraventricular mass. Most likely finding?

{{c1::Choroid plexus papilloma}}

1/2
fields.Extra:
Q20. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Large echogenic intraventricular mass most likely choroid plexus papilloma—benign tumor more common than carcinoma in fetal/neonatal period. Rapid growth typical in neonatal papillomas. Excellent prognosis with complete resection, but imaging cannot distinguish from malignant carcinoma prenatally. Though most cases are sporadic, risk factors include male sex, genetic syndromes (Li-Fraumeni, Aicardi), and viral exposure.




Open review Q20

fields.Text: A large echogenic intraventricular mass in the fetus/neonate suggests {{c1::choroid plexus papilloma}}.
fields.Extra:
Q20. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Large echogenic intraventricular mass most likely choroid plexus papilloma—benign tumor more common than carcinoma in fetal/neonatal period. Rapid growth typical in neonatal papillomas. Excellent prognosis with complete resection, but imaging cannot distinguish from malignant carcinoma prenatally. Though most cases are sporadic, risk factors include male sex, genetic syndromes (Li-Fraumeni, Aicardi), and viral exposure.






Open review Q20

fields.Text: Reading-room ambient light intensity is {{c1::illuminance}}, and the recommended maximum is about 50 lux.
fields.Extra:
Q21. Incorrect; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Maximum recommended reading room ambient lighting/illuminance is 50 lux. This should be comparable to standing under a streetlight at night.




Open review Q21

fields.Text: Ambient light intensity is called {{c1::illuminance}}.
fields.Extra:
Q21. Incorrect; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Maximum recommended reading room ambient lighting/illuminance is 50 lux. This should be comparable to standing under a streetlight at night.

fields.Text: NRC molybdenum breakthrough limit is {{c1::0.15 microcuries Mo-99 per 1 millicurie Tc-99m}} at administration.
fields.Extra:
Q22. Correct; difficulty: hard. Explanation: The Nuclear Regulatory Commission allows up to 0.15 microcuries of Mo-99 per 1 millicurie of Tc-99m at the time of administration to ensure radionuclide purity.




Open review Q22

fields.Text:
MAG3 scan with bilateral renal uptake but poor excretion in acute renal failure. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Acute tubular necrosis}}

1/2
fields.Extra:
Q23. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Progressive bilateral reniform tracer accumulation without excretion on MAG3 scan indicates severe bilateral renal dysfunction where kidneys retain perfusion and tubular uptake capacity but have lost excretory function—this pattern is characteristic of acute tubular necrosis. Normal renogram would also show bilateral reniform tracer accumulation, but should also show subsequent decrease of tracer as excretion occurs, usually seen within 3-5 minutes.




Open review Q23

fields.Text: MAG3 with preserved bilateral renal uptake but poor excretion suggests {{c1::acute tubular necrosis}}.
fields.Extra:
Q23. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Progressive bilateral reniform tracer accumulation without excretion on MAG3 scan indicates severe bilateral renal dysfunction where kidneys retain perfusion and tubular uptake capacity but have lost excretory function—this pattern is characteristic of acute tubular necrosis. Normal renogram would also show bilateral reniform tracer accumulation, but should also show subsequent decrease of tracer as excretion occurs, usually seen within 3-5 minutes.






Open review Q23

fields.Text:
Recurrent pancreatitis with dorsal duct drainage via minor papilla. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Pancreas divisum}}
fields.Extra:
Q24. Correct; difficulty: easy. Pancreas divisum is a failure of fusion of the dorsal and ventral pancreatic buds during embryological development.






Open review Q24

fields.Text: Pancreas divisum results from failed fusion of {{c1::dorsal and ventral pancreatic buds}}.
fields.Extra:
Q24. Correct; difficulty: easy. Pancreas divisum is a failure of fusion of the dorsal and ventral pancreatic buds during embryological development.

{{c1::Non-accidental trauma}}

Q25. Incorrect; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Radionuclide scans can be useful in locating sites of fractures and other injuries.

fields.Text:
Fall on outstretched hand with dorsal capitate dislocation and maintained radiolunate alignment. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Perilunate dislocation}}
fields.Extra:
Q26. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Trans-scaphoid perilunate dislocation is best appreciated on lateral radiograph with dorsal dislocation of the capitate with respect to the lunate and maintained radiolunate articulation.





Open review Q26

fields.Text: Perilunate dislocation shows {{c1::dorsal capitate dislocation relative to the lunate with preserved radiolunate alignment}}.
fields.Extra:

fields.Text:
Forefoot pain with ovoid intermetatarsal MRI mass. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Morton neuroma}}
fields.Extra:
Q27. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Morton's neuroma appears as an ovoid mass with low to intermediate signal on all MRI sequences, reflecting perineural fibrosis. When there is superimposed inflammation/edema, increased T2 signal may be seen within this lesion and in the surrounding soft tissues, as in this case.




Open review Q27

fields.Text: Morton neuroma is an {{c1::ovoid low-to-intermediate signal intermetatarsal mass}} caused by perineural fibrosis.

When there is superimposed inflammation/edema, increased T2 signal may be seen within this lesion and in the surrounding soft tissues, as in this case.

fields.Text:
Invasive lobular carcinoma with multifocal MRI disease after positive margins. Next step?

{{c1::Re-excision}}
fields.Extra:
Q28. Incorrect; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: In invasive lobular carcinoma, preoperative MRI multifocality and non-mass enhancement predict positive resection margins, with multifocality being the strongest predictor. These features help guide surgical planning toward wider excision or mastectomy.

fields.Text:
Neurocysticercosis with symptomatic enhancing lesions and host reaction. Stage?

{{c1::Colloidal vesicular stage}}
fields.Extra:
Q29. Correct; difficulty: hard. There are four main stages of neurocysticercosis (also known as Escobar's pathological stages): vesicular: viable parasite with intact membrane and therefore no host reaction colloidal vesicular: parasite dies within 4-5 years untreated, or earlier with treatment and the cyst fluid becomes turbid.





Open review Q29

fields.Text: The colloidal vesicular stage of neurocysticercosis occurs when {{c1::the parasite dies and the cyst fluid becomes turbid with host inflammatory reaction}}.
fields.Extra:

There are four main stages of neurocysticercosis (also known as Escobar's pathological stages): vesicular: viable parasite with intact membrane and therefore no host reaction colloidal vesicular: parasite dies within 4-5 years untreated, or earlier with treatment and the cyst fluid becomes turbid.

fields.Text:
RUQ ultrasound with echogenic biliary duct focus and no portal-vein wall. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Choledocholithiasis}}
fields.Extra:
Q30. Correct; difficulty: hard. One can tell these are biliary ducts (yellow arrows) and not vessels because they do not have a hyperechoic wall (see the purple arrow on the right portal vein for comparison).





Open review Q30

fields.Text: Bile ducts on US lack the {{c1::hyperechoic wall}} seen with adjacent portal veins.
fields.Extra:
Q30. Correct; difficulty: hard. One can tell these are biliary ducts (yellow arrows) and not vessels because they do not have a hyperechoic wall (see the purple arrow on the right portal vein for comparison).

fields.Text:
Young patient with intermittent positional headaches and third ventricular hyperdense lesion. Management?

{{c1::Neurosurgical consultation}}
fields.Extra:
Q31. Incorrect; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: The classic triad of third ventricular hyperattenuating lesion, intermittent positional headaches, and foramen of Monro location strongly suggests colloid cyst, requiring MRI confirmation and neurosurgical evaluation due to risk of sudden neurological deterioration from acute CSF obstruction.

fields.Text: A third-ventricular hyperattenuating lesion at the foramen of Monro with positional headaches suggests {{c1::colloid cyst requiring neurosurgical evaluation}}.

fields.Text:
Elbow MRI after dislocation with medial ligament tear. Injured structure?

{{c1::Medial ulnar collateral ligament}}
fields.Extra:
Q32. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: The anterior bundle of the ulnar collateral ligament appears as a low signal band on coronal MRI extending from the medial epicondyle to the sublime tubercle. Complete tears demonstrate focal discontinuity, surrounding edema, and abnormal laxity, requiring evaluation in both coronal and axial planes to determine tear location and distinguish the UCL from adjacent flexor tendons.




Open review Q32

fields.Text: The anterior bundle of the medial UCL runs from the {{c1::medial epicondyle to the sublime tubercle}}.
fields.Extra:
Q32. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: The anterior bundle of the ulnar collateral ligament appears as a low signal band on coronal MRI extending from the medial epicondyle to the sublime tubercle. Complete tears demonstrate focal discontinuity, surrounding edema, and abnormal laxity, requiring evaluation in both coronal and axial planes to determine tear location and distinguish the UCL from adjacent flexor tendons.





Open review Q32

fields.Text: Phase contrast cardiac MRI quantifies {{c1::blood flow, shunt fraction, and regurgitant fraction}}.
fields.Extra:
Q33. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Phase contrast cardiac MRI is used to image blood flow dynamics. It can be used to calculate pulmonary and systemic blood flow, determine shunt fractions, as well as calculate regurgitant fractions.




Open review Q33

fields.Text:
Chronic posterior heel pain with Achilles tendinopathy and retrocalcaneal/retro-Achilles bursitis. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Haglund syndrome}}

1/2
fields.Extra:
Q34. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: This patient has Haglund's syndrome. It refers to a triad of distal insertional Achilles tendinopathy (blue arrow), retro-calcaneal bursitis (red arrow), and retro Achilles bursitis (green arrow). Insertional Achilles tendinopathy can have a very high signal on T2WI or STIR and simulate a tendon tear. The pain is typically 2 to 3 cm proximal to Achilles tendon insertion.





Open review Q34

fields.Text: Haglund syndrome is the triad of {{c1::insertional Achilles tendinopathy, retrocalcaneal bursitis, and retro-Achilles bursitis}}.
fields.Extra:
Q34. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: This patient has Haglund's syndrome. It refers to a triad of distal insertional Achilles tendinopathy (blue arrow), retro-calcaneal bursitis (red arrow), and retro Achilles bursitis (green arrow). Insertional Achilles tendinopathy can have a very high signal on T2WI or STIR and simulate a tendon tear. The pain is typically 2 to 3 cm proximal to Achilles tendon insertion.

Nuclear medicine spectrum with I-123 photopeak. Radiopharmaceutical?

{{c1::I-123}}
fields.Extra:
Q35. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: It is important to be familiar with common photopeaks in nuclear imaging; many of these are listed in Appendix 3 of the RISC Study Guide.

fields.Text:
Post-trauma peritrochanteric fusiform fluid collection between subcutaneous fat and fascia. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Morel-Lavallee lesion}}
fields.Extra:
Q36. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Morel-Lavallée lesion appears as fusiform fluid collection between subcutaneous fat and fascia, most commonly peritrochanteric, with variable MRI signal based on blood products and characteristic internal septations and fat globules from shearing injury.




Open review Q36

fields.Text: Morel-Lavallee lesion is a {{c1::closed degloving injury with fluid between subcutaneous fat and fascia}}.
fields.Extra:
Q36. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Morel-Lavallée lesion appears as fusiform fluid collection between subcutaneous fat and fascia, most commonly peritrochanteric, with variable MRI signal based on blood products and characteristic internal septations and fat globules from shearing injury.

fields.Text: Creating a shared tool to improve call experience reflects commitment to {{c1::improving quality of care}}.
fields.Extra:
Q37. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: The medical profession adheres to fundamental principles and the achievement of definitive professional responsibilities. These are improving quality of care, scientific knowledge, professional competence, appropriate distribution of finite resources, and professional responsibilities.




Open review Q37

fields.Text:
Palpable testicular mass in young man. Diagnosis and next staging step?

{{c1::Testicular seminoma; retroperitoneal CT}}

1/2
fields.Extra:
Q38. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Retroperitoneal CT is essential after detecting a testicular mass because retroperitoneal lymph nodes are the primary metastatic site, and accurate nodal staging (stage I vs II/III) determines treatment approach. Nodes 8mm or larger are considered suspicious, with right testis draining to interaortocaval region and left testis to paraaortic nodes.




Open review Q38

fields.Text: After detecting testicular seminoma, evaluate the {{c1::retroperitoneum}} because it is the primary nodal metastatic route.
fields.Extra:
Q38. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Retroperitoneal CT is essential after detecting a testicular mass because retroperitoneal lymph nodes are the primary metastatic site, and accurate nodal staging (stage I vs II/III) determines treatment approach. Nodes 8mm or larger are considered suspicious, with right testis draining to interaortocaval region and left testis to paraaortic nodes.






Open review Q38

fields.Text:
Cyclic pelvic pain with thickened junctional zone and myometrial microcysts. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Adenomyosis}}
fields.Extra:
Q39. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Adenomyosis appears as junctional zone thickening greater than 12 mm, ill-defined junctional zone margins, and microcysts within the myometrium appearing as T2 hyperintense foci.





Open review Q39

fields.Text: Adenomyosis shows {{c1::junctional zone thickening >12 mm, ill-defined margins, and myometrial T2 microcysts}}.

fields.Text:
Bronchial artery embolization. Most feared complication?

{{c1::Paralysis}}
fields.Extra:
Q40. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Bronchial artery embolization, pictured here, requires selection of the bronchial arteries (red arrows), which most commonly arise directly from the aorta to supply the non-gas-exchanging components of the lungs.





Open review Q40

fields.Text: The most feared complication of bronchial artery embolization is {{c1::spinal cord ischemia/paralysis}}.

fields.Text:
Corpus callosum lesion differential. Least likely process?

{{c1::Infarction}}
fields.Extra:
Q41. Incorrect; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Corpus callosum infarcts are rare due to rich collateral blood supply from anterior and posterior pericallosal arteries. When they occur, they are associated with systemic vasculitides, shower emboli, major ischemic stroke, or subfalcine herniation with mass effect, with the splenium most commonly affected, followed by the body and genu.




Open review Q41

fields.Text: Corpus callosum infarction is rare because of {{c1::rich collateral supply from anterior and posterior pericallosal arteries}}.

When they occur, they are associated with systemic vasculitides, shower emboli, major ischemic stroke, or subfalcine herniation with mass effect, with the splenium most commonly affected, followed by the body and genu.

fields.Text: Corpus callosum infarction is {{c1::uncommon}}, making infarction the least likely corpus-callosum process among common alternatives.

fields.Text: National Patient Safety Goals are established by {{c1::The Joint Commission}}.
fields.Extra:
Q42. Incorrect; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Joint Commission National Patient Safety Goals mandate using two patient identifiers (name and date of birth), timely communication of critical test results to appropriate staff, and Universal Protocol for procedures (correct patient, correct site marking, time out before procedure). These apply across all healthcare settings and are fundamental to safety requirements.

fields.Text:
HIV patient with flank pain, radiolucent stone on noncontrast CT, filling defect on delayed phase. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Indinavir stone}}

1/3
fields.Extra:
Q43. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: The lifetime incidence of renal stones is high, seen in as many as 5% of females and 12% of males. By far the most common stone is calcium oxalate, however, the exact distribution of stones depends on the population and associated metabolic abnormalities.

fields.Text:
Breast US with abscess. Next step?

{{c1::Ultrasound-guided aspiration and antibiotics}}

1/2
fields.Extra:
Q44. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Breast abscess shows hypoechoic collection with thick echogenic rim and peripheral vascularity on ultrasound. First-line treatment is ultrasound-guided aspiration and antibiotic treatment.

fields.Text:
Older patient with sudden knee pain and subchondral hypointense lines with marrow edema. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Subchondral insufficiency fractures}}
fields.Extra:
Q45. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Insufficiency fractures presents with a subchondral hypointense line parallel to the articular surface with extensive bone marrow edema in older patients with sudden knee pain. Posterior meniscal root tears are commonly associated.

fields.Text:
Cardiac cine MRI sequence for wall motion/function. Sequence?

{{c1::SSFP}}
fields.Extra:
Q46. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: When you see "cine imaging for cardiac function" or "wall motion assessment," think SSFP as the sequence of choice due to its superior and consistent blood-myocardium contrast.

{{c1::Treat headache medically; benign variant}}

fields.Text:
Cirrhosis with rounded distal esophageal/gastric filling defects. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Varices}}
fields.Extra:
Q48. Correct; difficulty: easy. The presence of well-rounded filling defects within the distal esophagus (blue arrow) and stomach (yellow arrow) suggests the presence of gastroesophageal varices in the setting of cirrhosis and portal hypertension.

fields.Text:
HIDA with persistent blood pool and poor hepatic uptake/nonvisualized ducts. Interpretation?

{{c1::Severe hepatocellular dysfunction}}
fields.Extra:
Q50. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: The HIDA pattern of persistent blood pool activity with poor hepatic uptake and non-visualized ducts indicates severe hepatocellular dysfunction, not biliary obstruction. In true biliary obstruction, hepatic uptake would be normal with a "persistent hepatogram" showing no excretion into ducts or bowel. This distinction is critical because hepatocellular dysfunction requires medical optimization of the underlying condition, while biliary obstruction requires urgent decompression.




Open review Q50

fields.Text: HIDA persistent blood pool with poor hepatic uptake and no duct visualization indicates {{c1::severe hepatocellular dysfunction}}, not primary biliary obstruction.
fields.Extra:

In true biliary obstruction, hepatic uptake would be normal with a "persistent hepatogram" showing no excretion into ducts or bowel. This distinction is critical because hepatocellular dysfunction requires medical optimization of the underlying condition, while biliary obstruction requires urgent decompression.

fields.Text:
Adolescent kicking injury with AIIS marrow edema. Tendon involved?

{{c1::Rectus femoris}}
fields.Extra:
Q51. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Increased fluid signal in the anterior inferior iliac spine on magnetic resonance imaging indicates bone marrow edema from rectus femoris avulsion injury or stress reaction, commonly seen in adolescent soccer players from kicking motions.





Open review Q51

fields.Text: The rectus femoris originates from the {{c1::anterior inferior iliac spine}}.

1/3

Q52. Correct; difficulty: moderate. The CT shows non-aggressive expansion of the petrous apex by a soft tissue mass, which on the T2-weighted MR is hyperintense mass.

fields.Text:
Sinus of Valsalva aneurysm. Most commonly involved sinus?

{{c1::Right coronary/anterior sinus}}
fields.Extra:
Q53. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: A sinus of Valsalva aneurysm refers to an abnormal dilatation, congenital or acquired, of the sinus of Valsalva arising from one of the aortic sinuses. A sinus of Valsalva aneurysm is a cause of thoracic aortic dilatation. They most often involve the right coronary (anterior) sinus, less frequently the non-coronary (right posterior) sinus, and rarely involve the left coronary (left posterior) sinus.




Open review Q53

fields.Text: Sinus of Valsalva aneurysm most commonly involves the {{c1::right coronary/anterior sinus}}.

They most often involve the right coronary (anterior) sinus, less frequently the non-coronary (right posterior) sinus, and rarely involve the left coronary (left posterior) sinus.

fields.Text:
Young woman with resistant hypertension and beaded renal arteries. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Fibromuscular dysplasia}}

1/2
fields.Extra:
Q54. Correct; difficulty: easy. The CT angiographic beaded appearance of the renal arteries in this case (red arrows) is characteristic for the diagnosis of fibromuscular dysplasia (FMD), which is a common cause of treatment-resistant hypertension in young patients.

fields.Text: Fibromuscular dysplasia causes {{c1::beaded renal arteries}} and can cause resistant hypertension in young women.

fields.Text:
Recurrent left lower extremity DVT with left common iliac vein compression/collaterals. Diagnosis?

{{c1::May-Thurner syndrome}}
fields.Extra:
Q55. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Venographic findings show good contrast opacification in peripheral common iliac vein with poor proximal filling toward IVC likely related to compression at arterial crossing. Early collateral formation is suggested, suspicious for hemodynamically significant compression.





Open review Q55

fields.Text: May-Thurner syndrome is compression of the {{c1::left common iliac vein by the right common iliac artery}}.
